revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Attempting to force refresh]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Yamato-hiko no Mikoto''' (倭彦命) (date of birth unknown - 10th day of the 10th month, 28th year of Emperor Suinin's reign) was a prince of ancient [[Japan]] as recorded in historical texts such as the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]]. In the ''Nihon Shoki'', he is referred to as "Yamato-hiko no Mikoto," in the ''Kojiki'' as "'''Yamato-hiko no Mikoto''' (倭日子命)," and in other texts as "'''Yamato-hiko no Ōji''' (倭彦王子)"''Zoku Nihongi,'' Emperor Ten'ō, first year (781), entry for the 25th day of the 6th month.. He was a son of the 10th Emperor, [[Emperor Sujin]], and a younger brother of Emperor [[Suinin]], the 11th Emperor. The ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki'' do not record any achievements or descendants but provide details regarding his funeral. == Records == According to the ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki,'' Yamato-hiko no Mikoto was born to [[Emperor Sujin]] and Empress [[Mimaki-hime]]. His elder brother was Ikumeiribikoisachi no Mikoto, later known as Emperor [[Suinin]]. The ''Nihon Shoki'' records that Yamato-hiko no Mikoto passed away on the 10th day of the 10th month in the 28th year of Emperor [[Suinin]]'s reign and was buried on the 2nd day of the 11th month at "Musano Tsukisaka." At his burial, attendants were interred alive around the grave. These individuals survived for several days, groaning continuously day and night, and their bodies were later scavenged by dogs and birds after death. Emperor [[Suinin]], moved by pity, issued a decree banning the practice of [[junshi]] (ritual suicide) with the words, "Even if this is an ancient custom, what good is it if it is not proper?"{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}. Furthermore, the ''Nihon Shoki'' mentions that when Empress [[Hibasu-hime|Hibasuhime no Mikoto]] died in the 32nd year of Emperor Suinin's reign, [[Nomi no Sukune]] suggested replacing human and animal sacrifices with clay objects for burial. This suggestion delighted the Emperor and became the origin of the practice of creating [[haniwa]] (clay figures), specifically the anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa used in burials.{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}. The ''Kojiki'' briefly notes that at the burial of Yamato-hiko no Mikoto (倭日子命), human sacrifices were performed for the first time, stating, "At this king's funeral, a human fence was erected."{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}. A similar tradition is recorded in the ''Zoku Nihongi'' under the year Ten'ō 1 (781). == Tomb == [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Tomb of Yamato-hiko no Mikoto
[[Masuyama Kofun]]
([[Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]])}]]] The [[Japanese imperial tombs|grave]] is designated by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as ''Musano Tsukisaka no Haka'' (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) located in Toriya-chō, [[Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]].{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} The archaeological site name is [[Masuyama Kofun]]. The tomb is classified by the Imperial Household Agency as a square mound. According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', Yamato-hiko no Mikoto was buried at "Musano Tsukisaka," as noted above, but it is not listed in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' under the Bureau of Mausolea.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} In the [[Edo period]], the stone chamber to the east of {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|ja|鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}} was also considered to be his grave.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} In April 1877, during the [[Meiji period]], the Ministry of Home Affairs officially designated the current site as his grave, and the area was acquired along with the relocation of a shrine. Repairs began in 1890.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} However, modern research often questions the validity of this designation. == Analysis == The ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with [[junshi]] (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition. Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}} The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Figural Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa}}, rather than about all [[haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}). Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} have roots in the [[Yayoi period]] in the [[Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa appear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 2011, pp. 77-78. == Notes == {{Notelist}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Musano Tsukisaka no Haka|title=[[Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Edition)|Kokushi Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=Yamato-hiko no Mikoto|title=Dictionary of Names of Ancient Japanese Clans, Standard Edition|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} * {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Prince Yamato-hiko no Mikoto, son of Emperor Sujin)] - Kashihara City Homepage, "Kashihara Explorer" [[Category:Male royalty before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yamatohiko}} [[Category:Yamatohiko]]{{citation needed|Yamatohiko}} [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yamatohiko}} [[Category:Yamatohiko]]{{citation needed|Yamatohiko}} [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yamatohiko}} [[Category:Yamatohiko]]{{citation needed|Yamatohiko}}[[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:last refresh attempt]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:倭彦命]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:amatohiko]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:cCompletion tier 5]][[Category:tier 5]][[Category: 5]]]]

Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} have roots in the [[Yayoi period]] in the [[Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa appear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 2011, pp. 77-78. == Notes == {{Notelist}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Musano Tsukisaka no Haka|title=[[Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Edition)|Kokushi Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=Yamato-hiko no Mikoto|title=Dictionary of Names of Ancient Japanese Clans, Standard Edition|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} * {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Prince Yamato-hiko no Mikoto, son of Emperor Sujin)] - '''Yamato-hiko no Mikoto''' (倭彦命) (date of birth unknown - 10th day of the 10th month, 28th year of Emperor Suinin's reign) was a prince of ancient [[Japan]] as recorded in historical texts such as the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]]. In the ''Nihon Shoki'', he is referred to as "Yamato-hiko no Mikoto," in the ''Kojiki'' as "'''Yamato-hiko no Mikoto''' (倭日子命)," and in other texts as "'''Yamato-hiko no Ōji''' (倭彦王子)"''Zoku Nihongi,'' Emperor Ten'ō, first year (781), entry for the 25th day of the 6th month.. He was a son of the 10th Emperor, [[Emperor Sujin]], and a younger brother of Emperor [[Suinin]], the 11th Emperor. The ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki'' do not record any achievements or descendants but provide details regarding his funeral. == Records == According to the ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki,'' Yamato-hiko no Mikoto was born to [[Emperor Sujin]] and Empress [[Mimaki-hime]]. His elder brother was Ikumeiribikoisachi no Mikoto, later known as Emperor [[Suinin]]. The ''Nihon Shoki'' records that Yamato-hiko no Mikoto passed away on the 10th day of the 10th month in the 28th year of Emperor [[Suinin]]'s reign and was buried on the 2nd day of the 11th month at "Musano Tsukisaka." At his burial, attendants were interred alive around the grave. These individuals survived for several days, groaning continuously day and night, and their bodies were later scavenged by dogs and birds after death. Emperor [[Suinin]], moved by pity, issued a decree banning the practice of [[junshi]] (ritual suicide) with the words, "Even if this is an ancient custom, what good is it if it is not proper?"{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}. Furthermore, the ''Nihon Shoki'' mentions that when Empress [[Hibasu-hime|Hibasuhime no Mikoto]] died in the 32nd year of Emperor Suinin's reign, [[Nomi no Sukune]] suggested replacing human and animal sacrifices with clay objects for burial. This suggestion delighted the Emperor and became the origin of the practice of creating [[haniwa]] (clay figures), specifically the anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa used in burials.{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}. The ''Kojiki'' briefly notes that at the burial of Yamato-hiko no Mikoto (倭日子命), human sacrifices were performed for the first time, stating, "At this king's funeral, a human fence was erected."{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}. A similar tradition is recorded in the ''Zoku Nihongi'' under the year Ten'ō 1 (781). == Tomb == [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Tomb of Yamato-hiko no Mikoto
[[Masuyama Kofun]]
([[Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]])}]]] The [[Japanese imperial tombs|grave]] is designated by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as ''Musano Tsukisaka no Haka'' (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) located in Toriya-chō, [[Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]].{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} The archaeological site name is [[Masuyama Kofun]]. The tomb is classified by the Imperial Household Agency as a square mound. According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', Yamato-hiko no Mikoto was buried at "Musano Tsukisaka," as noted above, but it is not listed in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' under the Bureau of Mausolea.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} In the [[Edo period]], the stone chamber to the east of {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|ja|鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}} was also considered to be his grave.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} In April 1877, during the [[Meiji period]], the Ministry of Home Affairs officially designated the current site as his grave, and the area was acquired along with the relocation of a shrine. Repairs began in 1890.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} However, modern research often questions the validity of this designation. == Analysis == The ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with [[junshi]] (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition. Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}} The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Figural Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa}}, rather than about all [[haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}). Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} have roots in the [[Yayoi period]] in the [[Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa appear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 2011, pp. 77-78. == Notes == {{Notelist}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Musano Tsukisaka no Haka|title=[[Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Edition)|Kokushi Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=Yamato-hiko no Mikoto|title=Dictionary of Names of Ancient Japanese Clans, Standard Edition|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} * {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Prince Yamato-hiko no Mikoto, son of Emperor Sujin)] - '''Yamato-hiko no Mikoto''' (倭彦命) (date of birth unknown - 10th day of the 10th month, 28th year of Emperor Suinin's reign) was a prince of ancient [[Japan]] as recorded in historical texts such as the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]]. In the ''Nihon Shoki'', he is referred to as "Yamato-hiko no Mikoto," in the ''Kojiki'' as "'''Yamato-hiko no Mikoto''' (倭日子命)," and in other texts as "'''Yamato-hiko no Ōji''' (倭彦王子)"''Zoku Nihongi,'' Emperor Ten'ō, first year (781), entry for the 25th day of the 6th month.. He was a son of the 10th Emperor, [[Emperor Sujin]], and a younger brother of Emperor [[Suinin]], the 11th Emperor. The ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki'' do not record any achievements or descendants but provide details regarding his funeral. == Records == According to the ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki,'' Yamato-hiko no Mikoto was born to [[Emperor Sujin]] and Empress [[Mimaki-hime]]. His elder brother was Ikumeiribikoisachi no Mikoto, later known as Emperor [[Suinin]]. The ''Nihon Shoki'' records that Yamato-hiko no Mikoto passed away on the 10th day of the 10th month in the 28th year of Emperor [[Suinin]]'s reign and was buried on the 2nd day of the 11th month at "Musano Tsukisaka." At his burial, attendants were interred alive around the grave. These individuals survived for several days, groaning continuously day and night, and their bodies were later scavenged by dogs and birds after death. Emperor [[Suinin]], moved by pity, issued a decree banning the practice of [[junshi]] (ritual suicide) with the words, "Even if this is an ancient custom, what good is it if it is not proper?"{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}. Furthermore, the ''Nihon Shoki'' mentions that when Empress [[Hibasu-hime|Hibasuhime no Mikoto]] died in the 32nd year of Emperor Suinin's reign, [[Nomi no Sukune]] suggested replacing human and animal sacrifices with clay objects for burial. This suggestion delighted the Emperor and became the origin of the practice of creating [[haniwa]] (clay figures), specifically the anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa used in burials.{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}. The ''Kojiki'' briefly notes that at the burial of Yamato-hiko no Mikoto (倭日子命), human sacrifices were performed for the first time, stating, "At this king's funeral, a human fence was erected."{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}. A similar tradition is recorded in the ''Zoku Nihongi'' under the year Ten'ō 1 (781). == Tomb == [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Tomb of Yamato-hiko no Mikoto
[[Masuyama Kofun]]
([[Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]])}]]] The [[Japanese imperial tombs|grave]] is designated by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as ''Musano Tsukisaka no Haka'' (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) located in Toriya-chō, [[Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]].{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} The archaeological site name is [[Masuyama Kofun]]. The tomb is classified by the Imperial Household Agency as a square mound. According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', Yamato-hiko no Mikoto was buried at "Musano Tsukisaka," as noted above, but it is not listed in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' under the Bureau of Mausolea.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} In the [[Edo period]], the stone chamber to the east of {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|ja|鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}} was also considered to be his grave.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} In April 1877, during the [[Meiji period]], the Ministry of Home Affairs officially designated the current site as his grave, and the area was acquired along with the relocation of a shrine. Repairs began in 1890.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} However, modern research often questions the validity of this designation. == Analysis == The ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with [[junshi]] (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition. Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}} The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Figural Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa}}, rather than about all [[haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}). Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} have roots in the [[Yayoi period]] in the [[Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa appear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 2011, pp. 77-78. == Notes == {{Notelist}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Musano Tsukisaka no Haka|title=[[Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Edition)|Kokushi Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=Yamato-hiko no Mikoto|title=Dictionary of Names of Ancient Japanese Clans, Standard Edition|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} * {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Prince Yamato-hiko no Mikoto, son of Emperor Sujin)] - revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Yamato-hiko no Mikoto''' (倭彦命) (date of birth unknown - 10th day of the 10th month, 28th year of Emperor Suinin's reign) was a prince of ancient [[Japan]] as recorded in historical texts such as the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]]. In the ''Nihon Shoki'', he is referred to as "Yamato-hiko no Mikoto," in the ''Kojiki'' as "'''Yamato-hiko no Mikoto''' (倭日子命)," and in other texts as "'''Yamato-hiko no Ōji''' (倭彦王子)"''Zoku Nihongi,'' Emperor Ten'ō, first year (781), entry for the 25th day of the 6th month.. He was a son of the 10th Emperor, [[Emperor Sujin]], and a younger brother of Emperor [[Suinin]], the 11th Emperor. The ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki'' do not record any achievements or descendants but provide details regarding his funeral. == Records == According to the ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki,'' Yamato-hiko no Mikoto was born to [[Emperor Sujin]] and Empress [[Mimaki-hime]]. His elder brother was Ikumeiribikoisachi no Mikoto, later known as Emperor [[Suinin]]. The ''Nihon Shoki'' records that Yamato-hiko no Mikoto passed away on the 10th day of the 10th month in the 28th year of Emperor [[Suinin]]'s reign and was buried on the 2nd day of the 11th month at "Musano Tsukisaka." At his burial, attendants were interred alive around the grave. These individuals survived for several days, groaning continuously day and night, and their bodies were later scavenged by dogs and birds after death. Emperor [[Suinin]], moved by pity, issued a decree banning the practice of [[junshi]] (ritual suicide) with the words, "Even if this is an ancient custom, what good is it if it is not proper?"{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}. Furthermore, the ''Nihon Shoki'' mentions that when Empress [[Hibasu-hime|Hibasuhime no Mikoto]] died in the 32nd year of Emperor Suinin's reign, [[Nomi no Sukune]] suggested replacing human and animal sacrifices with clay objects for burial. This suggestion delighted the Emperor and became the origin of the practice of creating [[haniwa]] (clay figures), specifically the anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa used in burials.{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}. The ''Kojiki'' briefly notes that at the burial of Yamato-hiko no Mikoto (倭日子命), human sacrifices were performed for the first time, stating, "At this king's funeral, a human fence was erected."{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}. A similar tradition is recorded in the ''Zoku Nihongi'' under the year Ten'ō 1 (781). == Tomb == [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Tomb of Yamato-hiko no Mikoto
[[Masuyama Kofun]]
([[Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]])}]]] The [[Japanese imperial tombs|grave]] is designated by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as ''Musano Tsukisaka no Haka'' (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) located in Toriya-chō, [[Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]].{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} The archaeological site name is [[Masuyama Kofun]]. The tomb is classified by the Imperial Household Agency as a square mound. According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', Yamato-hiko no Mikoto was buried at "Musano Tsukisaka," as noted above, but it is not listed in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' under the Bureau of Mausolea.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} In the [[Edo period]], the stone chamber to the east of {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|ja|鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}} was also considered to be his grave.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} In April 1877, during the [[Meiji period]], the Ministry of Home Affairs officially designated the current site as his grave, and the area was acquired along with the relocation of a shrine. Repairs began in 1890.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} However, modern research often questions the validity of this designation. == Analysis == The ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with [[junshi]] (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition. Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}} The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Figural Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa}}, rather than about all [[haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}). Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} have roots in the [[Yayoi period]] in the [[Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa appear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 2011, pp. 77-78. == Notes == {{Notelist}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Musano Tsukisaka no Haka|title=[[Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Edition)|Kokushi Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=Yamato-hiko no Mikoto|title=Dictionary of Names of Ancient Japanese Clans, Standard Edition|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} * {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Prince Yamato-hiko no Mikoto, son of Emperor Sujin)] - Kashihara City Homepage, "Kashihara Explorer" [[Category:Male royalty before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] {{citation needed|Yamatohiko}} [[Category:Yamatohiko]]{{citation needed|Yamatohiko}} {{citation needed|Yamatohiko}} [[Category:Yamatohiko]]{{citation needed|Yamatohiko}} {{citation needed|Yamatohiko}} [[Category:Yamatohiko]]{{citation needed|Yamatohiko}}[[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:last refresh attempt]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:倭彦命]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:amatohiko]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:cCompletion tier 5]][[Category:tier 5]][[Category: 5]]]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]]

Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} have roots in the [[Yayoi period]] in the [[Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa appear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 2011, pp. 77-78. == Notes == {{Notelist}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Musano Tsukisaka no Haka|title=[[Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Edition)|Kokushi Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=Yamato-hiko no Mikoto|title=Dictionary of Names of Ancient Japanese Clans, Standard Edition|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} * {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Prince Yamato-hiko no Mikoto, son of Emperor Sujin)] - '''Yamato-hiko no Mikoto''' (倭彦命) (date of birth unknown - 10th day of the 10th month, 28th year of Emperor Suinin's reign) was a prince of ancient [[Japan]] as recorded in historical texts such as the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]]. In the ''Nihon Shoki'', he is referred to as "Yamato-hiko no Mikoto," in the ''Kojiki'' as "'''Yamato-hiko no Mikoto''' (倭日子命)," and in other texts as "'''Yamato-hiko no Ōji''' (倭彦王子)"''Zoku Nihongi,'' Emperor Ten'ō, first year (781), entry for the 25th day of the 6th month.. He was a son of the 10th Emperor, [[Emperor Sujin]], and a younger brother of Emperor [[Suinin]], the 11th Emperor. The ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki'' do not record any achievements or descendants but provide details regarding his funeral. == Records == According to the ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki,'' Yamato-hiko no Mikoto was born to [[Emperor Sujin]] and Empress [[Mimaki-hime]]. His elder brother was Ikumeiribikoisachi no Mikoto, later known as Emperor [[Suinin]]. The ''Nihon Shoki'' records that Yamato-hiko no Mikoto passed away on the 10th day of the 10th month in the 28th year of Emperor [[Suinin]]'s reign and was buried on the 2nd day of the 11th month at "Musano Tsukisaka." At his burial, attendants were interred alive around the grave. These individuals survived for several days, groaning continuously day and night, and their bodies were later scavenged by dogs and birds after death. Emperor [[Suinin]], moved by pity, issued a decree banning the practice of [[junshi]] (ritual suicide) with the words, "Even if this is an ancient custom, what good is it if it is not proper?"{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}. Furthermore, the ''Nihon Shoki'' mentions that when Empress [[Hibasu-hime|Hibasuhime no Mikoto]] died in the 32nd year of Emperor Suinin's reign, [[Nomi no Sukune]] suggested replacing human and animal sacrifices with clay objects for burial. This suggestion delighted the Emperor and became the origin of the practice of creating [[haniwa]] (clay figures), specifically the anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa used in burials.{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}. The ''Kojiki'' briefly notes that at the burial of Yamato-hiko no Mikoto (倭日子命), human sacrifices were performed for the first time, stating, "At this king's funeral, a human fence was erected."{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}. A similar tradition is recorded in the ''Zoku Nihongi'' under the year Ten'ō 1 (781). == Tomb == [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Tomb of Yamato-hiko no Mikoto
[[Masuyama Kofun]]
([[Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]])}]]] The [[Japanese imperial tombs|grave]] is designated by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as ''Musano Tsukisaka no Haka'' (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) located in Toriya-chō, [[Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]].{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} The archaeological site name is [[Masuyama Kofun]]. The tomb is classified by the Imperial Household Agency as a square mound. According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', Yamato-hiko no Mikoto was buried at "Musano Tsukisaka," as noted above, but it is not listed in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' under the Bureau of Mausolea.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} In the [[Edo period]], the stone chamber to the east of {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|ja|鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}} was also considered to be his grave.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} In April 1877, during the [[Meiji period]], the Ministry of Home Affairs officially designated the current site as his grave, and the area was acquired along with the relocation of a shrine. Repairs began in 1890.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} However, modern research often questions the validity of this designation. == Analysis == The ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with [[junshi]] (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition. Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}} The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Figural Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa}}, rather than about all [[haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}). Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} have roots in the [[Yayoi period]] in the [[Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa appear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 2011, pp. 77-78. == Notes == {{Notelist}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Musano Tsukisaka no Haka|title=[[Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Edition)|Kokushi Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=Yamato-hiko no Mikoto|title=Dictionary of Names of Ancient Japanese Clans, Standard Edition|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} * {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Prince Yamato-hiko no Mikoto, son of Emperor Sujin)] - Kashihara City Homepage, "Kashihara Explorer" [[Category:Male royalty before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] {{citation needed|Yamatohiko}} [[Category:Yamatohiko]]{{citation needed|Yamatohiko}} {{citation needed|Yamatohiko}} [[Category:Yamatohiko]]{{citation needed|Yamatohiko}} {{citation needed|Yamatohiko}} [[Category:Yamatohiko]]{{citation needed|Yamatohiko}}[[Category:last refresh attempt]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]] [[ja:倭彦命]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:amatohiko]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:cCompletion tier 5]][[Category: 5]]]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]]

There is some contradiction in these accounts.It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition. Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }} The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Figural Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa}}, rather than about all [[haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}). Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} have roots in the [[Yayoi period]] in the [[Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa appear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 2011, pp. 77-78. == Notes == {{Notelist}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Musano Tsukisaka no Haka|title=[[Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Edition)|Kokushi Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=Yamato-hiko no Mikoto|title=Dictionary of Names of Ancient Japanese Clans, Standard Edition|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} * {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Prince Yamato-hiko no Mikoto, son of Emperor Sujin)] - Kashihara City Homepage, "Kashihara Explorer" [[Category:Male royalty before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] {{citation needed|Yamatohiko}} [[Category:Yamatohiko]]{{citation needed|Yamatohiko}} {{citation needed|Yamatohiko}} [[Category:Yamatohiko]]{{citation needed|Yamatohiko}} {{citation needed|Yamatohiko}} [[Category:Yamatohiko]]{{citation needed|Yamatohiko}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]] [[ja:倭彦命]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:amatohiko]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:cCompletion tier 5]][[Category: 5]]]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:11]]

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|崇神天皇の皇子|仲哀天皇五世孫|倭彦王}} '''倭彦命'''（やまとひこのみこと{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}、生年不詳 - [[垂仁天皇]]28年[[10月5日 (旧暦)|10月5日]]）は、[[記紀]]等に伝わる古代[[日本]]の[[皇族]]（王族）。 『[[日本書紀]]』では「倭彦命」、『[[古事記]]』では「'''倭日子命'''」、他文献では「'''倭彦王子'''」『続日本紀』天応元年（781年）6月壬子（25日）条。とも表記される。 第10代[[崇神天皇]]の[[皇子]]で、第11代[[垂仁天皇]]の同母弟である。『日本書紀』・『古事記』とも事績と子孫の記載はなく、葬儀についてのみ記している。 == 記録 == 『[[日本書紀]]』・『[[古事記]]』によれば、第10代[[崇神天皇]]と、[[皇后]]の[[御間城姫]]との間に生まれた[[皇子]]である。同母兄として活目入彦五十狹茅尊（第11代[[垂仁天皇]]）がいる。 『日本書紀』によれば、垂仁天皇28年[[10月5日 (旧暦)|10月5日]]に倭彦命は薨去し、11月2日に「身狭桃花鳥坂（むさのつきさか）」に葬られた。その際、近習は墓の周辺に生き埋めにされたが（[[日本書紀]]に記される初にして唯一の殉葬の記録である）、数日間も死なずに昼夜呻き続けたうえ、その死後には犬や鳥が腐肉を漁った。これを哀れんだ天皇は「古の風といえども、良からずは何ぞ従わん」と[[殉死]]の禁令を出したという{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}。 また同書垂仁天皇32年7月6日条では、皇后の[[日葉酢媛命]]（ひばすひめのみこと）が薨去した際、[[野見宿禰]]が人・馬などの土物（はにもの）を墓に立てて代替とすることを進言し、天皇は大いによろこび、以後これが慣例になったとする（人物埴輪・[[形象埴輪]]の起源譚）。この起源譚は、垂仁28年条の記事が前提になる{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}。 『古事記』では、倭日子命（倭彦命）の分注として、「この王の（葬儀の）時に、始めて陵に人垣を立てき（殉葬した）」としている{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}。 また、『[[続日本紀]]』[[天応 (日本)|天応]]元年（[[781年]]）条にも同様の伝承が記されている。 == 墓 == [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|倭彦命 [[桝山古墳|身狭桃花鳥坂墓]]
（[[奈良県]][[橿原市]]）}}]] [[陵墓|墓]]は、[[宮内庁]]により[[奈良県]][[橿原市]]鳥屋町にある'''身狭桃花鳥坂墓'''（身狹桃花鳥坂墓：むさのつきさかのはか、{{ウィキ座標|34|28|29.47|N|135|46|42.24|E|region:JP-29|位置|name=身狭桃花鳥坂墓（倭彦命墓）}}）に治定されている宮内省諸陵寮編[{{NDLDC|1237123/8}} 『陵墓要覧』]（1934年、国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション）8コマ。『宮内庁書陵部陵墓地形図集成』 学生社、1999年、巻末の「歴代順陵墓等一覧」表。{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}。宮内庁上の形式は方丘。遺跡名は「[[桝山古墳]]（ますやまこふん）」。 『日本書紀』では倭彦命は上記のように「身狭桃花鳥坂」に葬られた旨が記されているが、『[[延喜式]]』[[諸陵寮]]では記載を欠いている{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}。[[江戸時代]]には[[鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠]]東方の石室を倭彦命墓に比定する説もあった{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}。[[明治]]10年（[[1877年]]）4月に[[内務省 (日本)|内務省]]によって現在の墓に定められ、明治19年（[[1886年]]）に宮内省（現・[[宮内庁]]）によって用地買収とともに同地にあった神社が移転され、明治23年（[[1890年]]）から修営された{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}。しかし現在では治定に否定的な見解も強い。 == 考証 == 上記のように『日本書紀』『古事記』において、倭彦命には[[殉死]]との関わりが記されている。『日本書紀』では殉死を「古風（いにしえののり）」と記すが『古事記』では「始めて陵に人垣を立てき」とあり、この点で矛盾がある[[邪馬台国九州説]]により、倭の代表権を引き継いだ垂仁天皇が（[[ヤマト王権]]の伝統ではないが）卑弥呼の先例に従ったとして矛盾は無いと見ることも可能ではある。。日本における殉死習俗を知る他の記事としては、『[[魏志]]』[[魏志倭人伝|倭人伝]]に[[卑弥呼]]死去の際に「奴婢百余人」の殉葬が見えるほか、『[[日本書紀]]』[[大化]]2年（[[646年]]）3月25日条に殉死禁止の詔がある{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}。 なお、『日本書紀』の伝承はあくまで「人物埴輪・形象埴輪の起源譚」であって、「埴輪全般（[[円筒埴輪]]含む）の起源譚」とはならない点が注意される。考古学的にも、円筒埴輪は[[弥生時代]]の吉備地方で見られる[[特殊器台・特殊壺]]に淵源を持つ古い風習であるが、人物埴輪・形象埴輪は[[古墳時代]]中期から見られる風習になる（ただし前期にも人形土製品・石製品を墓に置く風習が稀に存在する）[[森浩一]] 『天皇陵古墳への招待（筑摩選書23）』 筑摩書房、2011年、pp. 77-78。。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=石井茂輔|year=|chapter=身狭桃花鳥坂墓|title=[[国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|国史大辞典]]|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=倭彦命|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}}} == 関連項目 == * [[崇神天皇]] * [[埴輪]] == 外部リンク == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html 桝山古墳（崇神天皇皇子倭彦命墓）] - 橿原市ホームページ「かしはら探訪ナビ」 {{DEFAULTSORT:やまとひこのみこと}} [[Category:古墳時代以前の男性皇族]] [[Category:生没年不詳]]

（[[奈良県]][[橿原市]]）}}]] [[陵墓|墓]]は、[[宮内庁]]により[[奈良県]][[橿原市]]鳥屋町にある'''身狭桃花鳥坂墓'''（身狹桃花鳥坂墓：むさのつきさかのはか、{{ウィキ座標|34|28|29.47|N|135|46|42.24|E|region:JP-29|位置|name=身狭桃花鳥坂墓（倭彦命墓）}}）に治定されている宮内省諸陵寮編[{{NDLDC|1237123/8}} 『陵墓要覧』]（1934年、国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション）8コマ。『宮内庁書陵部陵墓地形図集成』 学生社、1999年、巻末の「歴代順陵墓等一覧」表。{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}。宮内庁上の形式は方丘。遺跡名は「[[桝山古墳]]（ますやまこふん）」。 『日本書紀』では倭彦命は上記のように「身狭桃花鳥坂」に葬られた旨が記されているが、『[[延喜式]]』[[諸陵寮]]では記載を欠いている{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}。[[江戸時代]]には[[鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠]]東方の石室を倭彦命墓に比定する説もあった{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}。[[明治]]10年（[[1877年]]）4月に[[内務省 (日本)|内務省]]によって現在の墓に定められ、明治19年（[[1886年]]）に宮内省（現・[[宮内庁]]）によって用地買収とともに同地にあった神社が移転され、明治23年（[[1890年]]）から修営された{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}。しかし現在では治定に否定的な見解も強い。 == 考証 == 上記のように『日本書紀』『古事記』において、倭彦命には[[殉死]]との関わりが記されている。『日本書紀』では殉死を「古風（いにしえののり）」と記すが『古事記』では「始めて陵に人垣を立てき」とあり、この点で矛盾がある[[邪馬台国九州説]]により、倭の代表権を引き継いだ垂仁天皇が（[[ヤマト王権]]の伝統ではないが）卑弥呼の先例に従ったとして矛盾は無いと見ることも可能ではある。。日本における殉死習俗を知る他の記事としては、『[[魏志]]』[[魏志倭人伝|倭人伝]]に[[卑弥呼]]死去の際に「奴婢百余人」の殉葬が見えるほか、『[[日本書紀]]』[[大化]]2年（[[646年]]）3月25日条に殉死禁止の詔がある{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}。 なお、『日本書紀』の伝承はあくまで「人物埴輪・形象埴輪の起源譚」であって、「埴輪全般（[[円筒埴輪]]含む）の起源譚」とはならない点が注意される。考古学的にも、円筒埴輪は[[弥生時代]]の吉備地方で見られる[[特殊器台・特殊壺]]に淵源を持つ古い風習であるが、人物埴輪・形象埴輪は[[古墳時代]]中期から見られる風習になる（ただし前期にも人形土製品・石製品を墓に置く風習が稀に存在する）[[森浩一]] 『天皇陵古墳への招待（筑摩選書23）』 筑摩書房、2011年、pp. 77-78。。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=石井茂輔|year=|chapter=身狭桃花鳥坂墓|title=[[国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|国史大辞典]]|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=倭彦命|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010年}}}} == 関連項目 == * [[崇神天皇]] * [[埴輪]] == 外部リンク == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html 桝山古墳（崇神天皇皇子倭彦命墓）] - 橿原市ホームページ「かしはら探訪ナビ」 {{DEFAULTSORT:やまとひこのみこと}} [[Category:古墳時代以前の男性皇族]] [[Category:生没年不詳]]

'''Yamato-hiko no Mikoto''' (倭彦命) (date of birth unknown - 10th day of the 10th month, 28th year of Emperor Suinin's reign) was a prince of ancient [[Japan]] as recorded in historical texts such as the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]]. In the ''Nihon Shoki'', he is referred to as "Yamato-hiko no Mikoto," in the ''Kojiki'' as "'''Yamato-hiko no Mikoto''' (倭日子命)," and in other texts as "'''Yamato-hiko no Ōji''' (倭彦王子)"''Zoku Nihongi,'' Emperor Ten'ō, first year (781), entry for the 25th day of the 6th month.. He was a son of the 10th Emperor, [[Emperor Sujin]], and a younger brother of Emperor [[Suinin]], the 11th Emperor. The ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki'' do not record any achievements or descendants but provide details regarding his funeral. == Records == According to the ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki,'' Yamato-hiko no Mikoto was born to [[Emperor Sujin]] and Empress [[Mimaki-hime]]. His elder brother was Ikumeiribikoisachi no Mikoto, later known as Emperor [[Suinin]]. The ''Nihon Shoki'' records that Yamato-hiko no Mikoto passed away on the 10th day of the 10th month in the 28th year of Emperor [[Suinin]]'s reign and was buried on the 2nd day of the 11th month at "Musano Tsukisaka." At his burial, attendants were interred alive around the grave. These individuals survived for several days, groaning continuously day and night, and their bodies were later scavenged by dogs and birds after death. Emperor [[Suinin]], moved by pity, issued a decree banning the practice of [[junshi]] (ritual suicide) with the words, "Even if this is an ancient custom, what good is it if it is not proper?"{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }}. Furthermore, the ''Nihon Shoki'' mentions that when Empress [[Hibasu-hime|Hibasuhime no Mikoto]] died in the 32nd year of Emperor Suinin's reign, [[Nomi no Sukune]] suggested replacing human and animal sacrifices with clay objects for burial. This suggestion delighted the Emperor and became the origin of the practice of creating [[haniwa]] (clay figures), specifically the anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa used in burials.{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }}. The ''Kojiki'' briefly notes that at the burial of Yamato-hiko no Mikoto (倭日子命), human sacrifices were performed for the first time, stating, "At this king's funeral, a human fence was erected."{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }}. A similar tradition is recorded in the ''Zoku Nihongi'' under the year Ten'ō 1 (781). == Tomb == [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Tomb of Yamato-hiko no Mikoto
[[Masuyama Kofun]]
([[Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]])}]]] The [[Japanese imperial tombs|grave]] is designated by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as ''Musano Tsukisaka no Haka'' (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) located in Toriya-chō, [[Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]].{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} The archaeological site name is [[Masuyama Kofun]]. The tomb is classified by the Imperial Household Agency as a square mound. According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', Yamato-hiko no Mikoto was buried at "Musano Tsukisaka," as noted above, but it is not listed in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' under the Bureau of Mausolea.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} In the [[Edo period]], the stone chamber to the east of {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|ja|鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was also considered to be his grave.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} In April 1877, during the [[Meiji period]], the Ministry of Home Affairs officially designated the current site as his grave, and the area was acquired along with the relocation of a shrine. Repairs began in 1890.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} However, modern research often questions the validity of this designation. == Analysis == The ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with [[junshi]] (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition. Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }} The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Figural Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, rather than about all [[haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=draft|qq=draft}} have roots in the [[Yayoi period]] in the [[Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa appear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 2011, pp. 77-78. == Notes == {{Notelist}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Musano Tsukisaka no Haka|title=[[Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Edition)|Kokushi Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=Yamato-hiko no Mikoto|title=Dictionary of Names of Ancient Japanese Clans, Standard Edition|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Prince Yamato-hiko no Mikoto, son of Emperor Sujin)] - Kashihara City Homepage, "Kashihara Explorer" {{translated page|ja|倭彦命|version=100295955|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Male royalty before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Yamatohiko]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:cCompletion tier 5]] }} [[sn:Yamatohiko]] [[en:Yamatohiko]] [[ja:倭彦命]] [[sn:amatohiko]]

revision.text:
'''Yamato-hiko no Mikoto''' (倭彦命) (date of birth unknown - 10th day of the 10th month, 28th year of Emperor Suinin's reign) was a prince of ancient [[Japan]] as recorded in historical texts such as the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]]. In the ''Nihon Shoki'', he is referred to as "Yamato-hiko no Mikoto," in the ''Kojiki'' as "'''Yamato-hiko no Mikoto''' (倭日子命)," and in other texts as "'''Yamato-hiko no Ōji''' (倭彦王子)"''Zoku Nihongi,'' Emperor Ten'ō, first year (781), entry for the 25th day of the 6th month.. He was a son of the 10th Emperor, [[Emperor Sujin]], and a younger brother of Emperor [[Suinin]], the 11th Emperor. The ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki'' do not record any achievements or descendants but provide details regarding his funeral. == Records == According to the ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki,'' Yamato-hiko no Mikoto was born to [[Emperor Sujin]] and Empress [[Mimaki-hime]]. His elder brother was Ikumeiribikoisachi no Mikoto, later known as Emperor [[Suinin]]. The ''Nihon Shoki'' records that Yamato-hiko no Mikoto passed away on the 10th day of the 10th month in the 28th year of Emperor [[Suinin]]'s reign and was buried on the 2nd day of the 11th month at "Musano Tsukisaka." At his burial, attendants were interred alive around the grave. These individuals survived for several days, groaning continuously day and night, and their bodies were later scavenged by dogs and birds after death. Emperor [[Suinin]], moved by pity, issued a decree banning the practice of [[junshi]] (ritual suicide) with the words, "Even if this is an ancient custom, what good is it if it is not proper?"{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }}. Furthermore, the ''Nihon Shoki'' mentions that when Empress [[Hibasu-hime|Hibasuhime no Mikoto]] died in the 32nd year of Emperor Suinin's reign, [[Nomi no Sukune]] suggested replacing human and animal sacrifices with clay objects for burial. This suggestion delighted the Emperor and became the origin of the practice of creating [[haniwa]] (clay figures), specifically the anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa used in burials.{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }}. The ''Kojiki'' briefly notes that at the burial of Yamato-hiko no Mikoto (倭日子命), human sacrifices were performed for the first time, stating, "At this king's funeral, a human fence was erected."{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }}. A similar tradition is recorded in the ''Zoku Nihongi'' under the year Ten'ō 1 (781). == Tomb == [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Tomb of Yamato-hiko no Mikoto
[[Masuyama Kofun]]
([[Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]])}]]] The [[Japanese imperial tombs|grave]] is designated by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as ''Musano Tsukisaka no Haka'' (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) located in Toriya-chō, [[Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]].{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} The archaeological site name is [[Masuyama Kofun]]. The tomb is classified by the Imperial Household Agency as a square mound. According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', Yamato-hiko no Mikoto was buried at "Musano Tsukisaka," as noted above, but it is not listed in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' under the Bureau of Mausolea.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} In the [[Edo period]], the stone chamber to the east of {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|ja|鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was also considered to be his grave.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} In April 1877, during the [[Meiji period]], the Ministry of Home Affairs officially designated the current site as his grave, and the area was acquired along with the relocation of a shrine. Repairs began in 1890.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} However, modern research often questions the validity of this designation. == Analysis == The ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with [[junshi]] (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition. Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }} The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Figural Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, rather than about all [[haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=draft|qq=draft}} have roots in the [[Yayoi period]] in the [[Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa appear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 2011, pp. 77-78. == Notes == {{Notelist}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Musano Tsukisaka no Haka|title=[[Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Edition)|Kokushi Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=Yamato-hiko no Mikoto|title=Dictionary of Names of Ancient Japanese Clans, Standard Edition|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Prince Yamato-hiko no Mikoto, son of Emperor Sujin)] - Kashihara City Homepage, "Kashihara Explorer" {{translated page|ja|倭彦命|version=100295955|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Male royalty before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Yamatohiko]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:cCgoodbye $1 5]] }} [[sn:Yamatohiko]] [[en:Yamatohiko]] [[ja:倭彦命]] [[sn:amatohiko]]

'''Yamato-hiko no Mikoto''' (倭彦命) (date of birth unknown - 10th day of the 10th month, 28th year of Emperor Suinin's reign) was a prince of ancient [[Japan]] as recorded in historical texts such as the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]]. In the ''Nihon Shoki'', he is referred to as "Yamato-hiko no Mikoto," in the ''Kojiki'' as "'''Yamato-hiko no Mikoto''' (倭日子命)," and in other texts as "'''Yamato-hiko no Ōji''' (倭彦王子)"''Zoku Nihongi,'' Emperor Ten'ō, first year (781), entry for the 25th day of the 6th month.. He was a son of the 10th Emperor, [[Emperor Sujin]], and a younger brother of Emperor [[Suinin]], the 11th Emperor. The ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki'' do not record any achievements or descendants but provide details regarding his funeral. == Records == According to the ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki,'' Yamato-hiko no Mikoto was born to [[Emperor Sujin]] and Empress [[Mimaki-hime]]. His elder brother was Ikumeiribikoisachi no Mikoto, later known as Emperor [[Suinin]]. The ''Nihon Shoki'' records that Yamato-hiko no Mikoto passed away on the 10th day of the 10th month in the 28th year of Emperor [[Suinin]]'s reign and was buried on the 2nd day of the 11th month at "Musano Tsukisaka." At his burial, attendants were interred alive around the grave. These individuals survived for several days, groaning continuously day and night, and their bodies were later scavenged by dogs and birds after death. Emperor [[Suinin]], moved by pity, issued a decree banning the practice of [[junshi]] (ritual suicide) with the words, "Even if this is an ancient custom, what good is it if it is not proper?"{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }}. Furthermore, the ''Nihon Shoki'' mentions that when Empress [[Hibasu-hime|Hibasuhime no Mikoto]] died in the 32nd year of Emperor Suinin's reign, [[Nomi no Sukune]] suggested replacing human and animal sacrifices with clay objects for burial. This suggestion delighted the Emperor and became the origin of the practice of creating [[haniwa]] (clay figures), specifically the anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa used in burials.{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }}. The ''Kojiki'' briefly notes that at the burial of Yamato-hiko no Mikoto (倭日子命), human sacrifices were performed for the first time, stating, "At this king's funeral, a human fence was erected."{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }}. A similar tradition is recorded in the ''Zoku Nihongi'' under the year Ten'ō 1 (781). == Tomb == [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Tomb of Yamato-hiko no Mikoto
[[Masuyama Kofun]]
([[Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]])}]]] The [[Japanese imperial tombs|grave]] is designated by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as ''Musano Tsukisaka no Haka'' (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) located in Toriya-chō, [[Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]].{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} The archaeological site name is [[Masuyama Kofun]]. The tomb is classified by the Imperial Household Agency as a square mound. According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', Yamato-hiko no Mikoto was buried at "Musano Tsukisaka," as noted above, but it is not listed in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' under the Bureau of Mausolea.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} In the [[Edo period]], the stone chamber to the east of {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|ja|鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was also considered to be his grave.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} In April 1877, during the [[Meiji period]], the Ministry of Home Affairs officially designated the current site as his grave, and the area was acquired along with the relocation of a shrine. Repairs began in 1890.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} However, modern research often questions the validity of this designation. == Analysis == The ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with [[junshi]] (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition. Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }} The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Figural Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, rather than about all [[haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=draft|qq=draft}} have roots in the [[Yayoi period]] in the [[Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa appear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 2011, pp. 77-78. == Notes == {{Notelist}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Musano Tsukisaka no Haka|title=[[Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Edition)|Kokushi Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=Yamato-hiko no Mikoto|title=Dictionary of Names of Ancient Japanese Clans, Standard Edition|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Prince Yamato-hiko no Mikoto, son of Emperor Sujin)] - Kashihara City Homepage, "Kashihara Explorer" [[Category:translated pages without meta categories]] {{translated page|ja|倭彦命|version=100295955|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Male royalty before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Yamatohiko]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:cCgoodbye $1 5]] }} [[sn:Yamatohiko]] [[en:Yamatohiko]] [[ja:倭彦命]] [[sn:amatohiko]]

[[Masuyama Kofun]]
([[Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]])}]]] The [[Japanese imperial tombs|grave]] is designated by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as ''Musano Tsukisaka no Haka'' (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) located in Toriya-chō, [[Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]].{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} The archaeological site name is [[Masuyama Kofun]]. Repairs began in 1890.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} However, modern research often questions the validity of this designation. == Analysis == The ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with [[junshi]] (ritual suicide). Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=draft|qq=draft}} have roots in the [[Yayoi period]] in the [[Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa appear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 2011, pp. 77-78. == Notes == {{Notelist}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Musano Tsukisaka no Haka|title=[[Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Edition)|Kokushi Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=Yamato-hiko no Mikoto|title=Dictionary of Names of Ancient Japanese Clans, Standard Edition|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Prince Yamato-hiko no Mikoto, son of Emperor Sujin)] - Kashihara City Homepage, "Kashihara Explorer" [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|倭彦命|version=100295955|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:Male royalty before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Yamatohiko]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:cCgoodbye $1 5]] }} [[sn:Yamatohiko]] [[en:Yamatohiko]] [[ja:倭彦命]] [[sn:amatohiko]]

'''Yamato-hiko no Mikoto''' (倭彦命) (date of birth unknown - 10th day of the 10th month, 28th year of Emperor Suinin's reign) was a prince of ancient [[Japan]] as recorded in historical texts such as the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]]. In the ''Nihon Shoki'', he is referred to as "Yamato-hiko no Mikoto," in the ''Kojiki'' as "'''Yamato-hiko no Mikoto''' (倭日子命)," and in other texts as "'''Yamato-hiko no Ōji''' (倭彦王子)"''Zoku Nihongi,'' Emperor Ten'ō, first year (781), entry for the 25th day of the 6th month.. He was a son of the 10th Emperor, [[Emperor Sujin]], and a younger brother of Emperor [[Suinin]], the 11th Emperor. The ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki'' do not record any achievements or descendants but provide details regarding his funeral. == Records == According to the ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki,'' Yamato-hiko no Mikoto was born to [[Emperor Sujin]] and Empress [[Mimaki-hime]]. His elder brother was Ikumeiribikoisachi no Mikoto, later known as Emperor [[Suinin]]. The ''Nihon Shoki'' records that Yamato-hiko no Mikoto passed away on the 10th day of the 10th month in the 28th year of Emperor [[Suinin]]'s reign and was buried on the 2nd day of the 11th month at "Musano Tsukisaka." At his burial, attendants were interred alive around the grave. These individuals survived for several days, groaning continuously day and night, and their bodies were later scavenged by dogs and birds after death. Emperor [[Suinin]], moved by pity, issued a decree banning the practice of [[junshi]] (ritual suicide) with the words, "Even if this is an ancient custom, what good is it if it is not proper?"{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }}. Furthermore, the ''Nihon Shoki'' mentions that when Empress [[Hibasu-hime|Hibasuhime no Mikoto]] died in the 32nd year of Emperor Suinin's reign, [[Nomi no Sukune]] suggested replacing human and animal sacrifices with clay objects for burial. This suggestion delighted the Emperor and became the origin of the practice of creating [[haniwa]] (clay figures), specifically the anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa used in burials.{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }}. The ''Kojiki'' briefly notes that at the burial of Yamato-hiko no Mikoto (倭日子命), human sacrifices were performed for the first time, stating, "At this king's funeral, a human fence was erected."{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }}. A similar tradition is recorded in the ''Zoku Nihongi'' under the year Ten'ō 1 (781). == Tomb == [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Tomb of Yamato-hiko no Mikoto
[[Masuyama Kofun]]
([[Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]])}]]] The [[Japanese imperial tombs|grave]] is designated by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as ''Musano Tsukisaka no Haka'' (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) located in Toriya-chō, [[Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]].{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} The archaeological site name is [[Masuyama Kofun]]. The tomb is classified by the Imperial Household Agency as a square mound. According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', Yamato-hiko no Mikoto was buried at "Musano Tsukisaka," as noted above, but it is not listed in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' under the Bureau of Mausolea.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} In the [[Edo period]], the stone chamber to the east of {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|ja|鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was also considered to be his grave.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} In April 1877, during the [[Meiji period]], the Ministry of Home Affairs officially designated the current site as his grave, and the area was acquired along with the relocation of a shrine. Repairs began in 1890.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} However, modern research often questions the validity of this designation. == Analysis == The ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with [[junshi]] (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition. Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }} The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Figural Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, rather than about all [[haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=draft|qq=draft}} have roots in the [[Yayoi period]] in the [[Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa appear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 2011, pp. 77-78. == Notes == {{Notelist}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Musano Tsukisaka no Haka|title=[[Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Edition)|Kokushi Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=Yamato-hiko no Mikoto|title=Dictionary of Names of Ancient Japanese Clans, Standard Edition|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Prince Yamato-hiko no Mikoto, son of Emperor Sujin)] - Kashihara City Homepage, "Kashihara Explorer" [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|倭彦命|version=100295955|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:Male royalty before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Yamatohiko]] [[Category:cCgoodbye $1 5]] }} [[sn:Yamatohiko]] [[en:Yamatohiko]] [[ja:倭彦命]] [[sn:amatohiko]]

Emperor [[Suinin]], moved by pity, issued a decree banning the practice of [[junshi]] (ritual suicide) with the words, "Even if this is an ancient custom, what good is it if it is not proper?"{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }}. Furthermore, the ''Nihon Shoki'' mentions that when Empress [[Hibasu-hime|Hibasuhime no Mikoto]] died in the 32nd year of Emperor Suinin's reign, [[Nomi no Sukune]] suggested replacing human and animal sacrifices with clay objects for burial. This suggestion delighted the Emperor and became the origin of the practice of creating [[haniwa]] (clay figures), specifically the anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa used in burials.{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }}. The ''Kojiki'' briefly notes that at the burial of Yamato-hiko no Mikoto (倭日子命), human sacrifices were performed for the first time, stating, "At this king's funeral, a human fence was erected."{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }}. A similar tradition is recorded in the ''Zoku Nihongi'' under the year Ten'ō 1 (781). == Tomb == [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Tomb of Yamato-hiko no Mikoto
[[Masuyama Kofun]]
([[Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]])}]]] The [[Japanese imperial tombs|grave]] is designated by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] as ''Musano Tsukisaka no Haka'' (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) located in Toriya-chō, [[Kashihara]], [[Nara Prefecture]].{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} The archaeological site name is [[Masuyama Kofun]]. The tomb is classified by the Imperial Household Agency as a square mound. According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', Yamato-hiko no Mikoto was buried at "Musano Tsukisaka," as noted above, but it is not listed in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' under the Bureau of Mausolea.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} In the [[Edo period]], the stone chamber to the east of {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|ja|鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|qq=}} was also considered to be his grave.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} In April 1877, during the [[Meiji period]], the Ministry of Home Affairs officially designated the current site as his grave, and the area was acquired along with the relocation of a shrine. Repairs began in 1890.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} However, modern research often questions the validity of this designation. == Analysis == The ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with [[junshi]] (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition. Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''[[Wajinden|Wei Zhi]]'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of [[Taika (era)|Taika]] (646).{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }} The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Figural Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Figural Haniwa|qq=}}, rather than about all [[haniwa]] (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}}). Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} have roots in the [[Yayoi period]] in the [[Kibi Province|Kibi region]] through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa appear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 2011, pp. 77-78. == Notes == {{Notelist}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Musano Tsukisaka no Haka|title=[[Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Edition)|Kokushi Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=Yamato-hiko no Mikoto|title=Dictionary of Names of Ancient Japanese Clans, Standard Edition|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }}}} == Related Articles == * [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] * [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Prince Yamato-hiko no Mikoto, son of Emperor Sujin)] - Kashihara City Homepage, "Kashihara Explorer" [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|倭彦命|version=100295955|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Yamatohiko]][[Category:Male royalty before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:cCgoodbye $1 5]] }} [[sn:Yamatohiko]] [[en:Yamatohiko]] [[ja:倭彦命]] [[sn:amatohiko]]
