There is some contradiction in these accounts.It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[:en:Suinin|Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition. Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''{{ill|Wajinden|en|Wajinden|WD=Q1135798|lt=Wei Zhi|1=Wajinden}}'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of {{ill|Taika (era)|en|Taika (era)|WD=Q1077930|lt=Taika|1=Taika (era)}} (646).{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }} The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Representational Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Representational Haniwa|qq=}}, rather than about all {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}} (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}}). Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} have roots in the {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|WD=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period|1=Yayoi period}} in the {{ill|Kibi Province|en|Kibi Province|WD=Q1155128|lt=Kibi region|1=Kingdom of Kibi}} through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa appear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 2011, pp. 77-78. == Notes == {{Notelist}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Musano Tsukisaka no Haka|title=[[Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Edition)|Kokushi Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=Yamato-hiko no Mikoto|title=Dictionary of Names of Ancient Japanese Clans, Standard Edition|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}} * {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Prince Yamato-hiko no Mikoto, son of Emperor Sujin)] - Kashihara City Homepage, "Kashihara Explorer" {{translated page|ja|倭彦命|version=100295955|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:倭彦命]] {{wikidata link|Q11387083}} [[Category:Male imperial family members before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]] [[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed curly braces]] [[Category:pages]]

[[ja:倭彦命]] [[ja:倭彦命]] [[ja:倭彦命]] {{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|father={{ill|Sujin|WD=Q333530|1=Emperor Sujin}}|mother={{ill|Empress Mimakihime|WD=Q11071228|1=Mimaki-hime}}|relatives={{ill|Prince Toyokiirihiko|WD=Q11633375|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko}}; {{ill|Princess Chichitsukuyamatohime|WD=Q10904824|1=Princess Chichitsukuyamatohime}}; {{ill|Princess Kunikatahime|WD=Q10926851|1=Princess Kunikatahime}}; {{ill|Princess Nunakiirihime|WD=Q11561867|1=Nunaki-iri-hime}}; {{ill|Princess Toochiniirihime|WD=Q10904619|1=Princess Toochiniirihime}}; {{ill|Suinin|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}; {{ill|Toyosukiirihime no mikoto|WD=Q10532771|1=Toyosukiiri-hime}}; {{ill|Yasaka no Irihiko no Mikoto|WD=Q14759414|1=Yasaka no Irihiko no Mikoto}}; {{ill|Yasakairihime no mikoto|WD=Q10892048|1=Yasakairi-hime}}}} '''Yamato-hiko no Mikoto''' (倭彦命) (date of birth unknown - 10th day of the 10th month, 28th year of Emperor Suinin's reign) was a prince of ancient {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} as recorded in historical texts such as the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]]. In the ''Nihon Shoki'', he is referred to as "Yamato-hiko no Mikoto," in the ''Kojiki'' as "'''Yamato-hiko no Mikoto''' (倭日子命)," and in other texts as "'''Yamato-hiko no Ōji''' (倭彦王子)"''Zoku Nihongi,'' Emperor Ten'ō, first year (781), entry for the 25th day of the 6th month.. He was a son of the 10th Emperor, {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}}, and a younger brother of Emperor [[:en:Suinin|Suinin]], the 11th Emperor. The ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki'' do not record any achievements or descendants but provide details regarding his funeral. == Records == According to the ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki,'' Yamato-hiko no Mikoto was born to {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}} and Empress [[Mimaki-hime]]. His elder brother was Ikumeiribikoisachi no Mikoto, later known as Emperor [[:en:Suinin|Suinin]]. The ''Nihon Shoki'' records that Yamato-hiko no Mikoto passed away on the 10th day of the 10th month in the 28th year of Emperor [[:en:Suinin|Suinin]]'s reign and was buried on the 2nd day of the 11th month at "Musano Tsukisaka." At his burial, attendants were interred alive around the grave. These individuals survived for several days, groaning continuously day and night, and their bodies were later scavenged by dogs and birds after death. Emperor [[:en:Suinin|Suinin]], moved by pity, issued a decree banning the practice of {{ill|Junshi|en|Junshi|WD=Q1071761|lt=Junshi|1=Junshi}} (ritual suicide) with the words, "Even if this is an ancient custom, what good is it if it is not proper?"{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }}. Furthermore, the ''Nihon Shoki'' mentions that when Empress [[Hibasu-hime|Hibasuhime no Mikoto]] died in the 32nd year of Emperor Suinin's reign, [[Nomi no Sukune]] suggested replacing human and animal sacrifices with clay objects for burial. This suggestion delighted the Emperor and became the origin of the practice of creating {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}} (clay figures), specifically the anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa used in burials.{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }}. The ''Kojiki'' briefly notes that at the burial of Yamato-hiko no Mikoto (倭日子命), human sacrifices were performed for the first time, stating, "At this king's funeral, a human fence was erected."{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }}. A similar tradition is recorded in the ''Zoku Nihongi'' under the year Ten'ō 1 (781). == Tomb == [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Tomb of Yamato-hiko no Mikoto
[[Masuyama Kofun]]
([[:en:Kashihara|Kashihara]], {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}})}]]] The [[Japanese imperial tombs|grave]] is designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} as ''Musano Tsukisaka no Haka'' (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) located in Toriya-chō, [[:en:Kashihara|Kashihara]], {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} The archaeological site name is [[Masuyama Kofun]]. The tomb is classified by the Imperial Household Agency as a square mound. According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', Yamato-hiko no Mikoto was buried at "Musano Tsukisaka," as noted above, but it is not listed in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' under the Bureau of Mausolea.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the stone chamber to the east of {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|ja|鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|qq=}} was also considered to be his grave.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} In April 1877, during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the Ministry of Home Affairs officially designated the current site as his grave, and the area was acquired along with the relocation of a shrine. Repairs began in 1890.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} However, modern research often questions the validity of this designation. == Analysis == The ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with {{ill|Junshi|en|Junshi|WD=Q1071761|lt=Junshi|1=Junshi}} (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[:en:Suinin|Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition. Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''{{ill|Wajinden|en|Wajinden|WD=Q1135798|lt=Wei Zhi|1=Wajinden}}'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of {{ill|Taika (era)|en|Taika (era)|WD=Q1077930|lt=Taika|1=Taika (era)}} (646).{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }} The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Representational Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Representational Haniwa|qq=}}, rather than about all {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}} (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}}). Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} have roots in the {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|WD=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period|1=Yayoi period}} in the {{ill|Kibi Province|en|Kibi Province|WD=Q1155128|lt=Kibi region|1=Kingdom of Kibi}} through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa appear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 2011, pp. 77-78. == Notes == {{Notelist}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Musano Tsukisaka no Haka|title=[[Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Edition)|Kokushi Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=Yamato-hiko no Mikoto|title=Dictionary of Names of Ancient Japanese Clans, Standard Edition|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}} * {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Prince Yamato-hiko no Mikoto, son of Emperor Sujin)] - Kashihara City Homepage, "Kashihara Explorer" {{translated page|ja|倭彦命|version=100295955|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:倭彦命]] {{wikidata link|Q11387083}} [[Category:Male imperial family members before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]] [[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed curly braces]]

[[ja:倭彦命]] [[ja:倭彦命]] [[ja:倭彦命]] {{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|father={{ill|Sujin|WD=Q333530|1=Emperor Sujin}}|mother={{ill|Empress Mimakihime|WD=Q11071228|1=Mimaki-hime}}|relatives={{ill|Prince Toyokiirihiko|WD=Q11633375|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko}}; {{ill|Princess Chichitsukuyamatohime|WD=Q10904824|1=Princess Chichitsukuyamatohime}}; {{ill|Princess Kunikatahime|WD=Q10926851|1=Princess Kunikatahime}}; {{ill|Princess Nunakiirihime|WD=Q11561867|1=Nunaki-iri-hime}}; {{ill|Princess Toochiniirihime|WD=Q10904619|1=Princess Toochiniirihime}}; {{ill|Suinin|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}; {{ill|Toyosukiirihime no mikoto|WD=Q10532771|1=Toyosukiiri-hime}}; {{ill|Yasaka no Irihiko no Mikoto|WD=Q14759414|1=Yasaka no Irihiko no Mikoto}}; {{ill|Yasakairihime no mikoto|WD=Q10892048|1=Yasakairi-hime}}}} '''Yamato-hiko no Mikoto''' (倭彦命) (date of birth unknown - 10th day of the 10th month, 28th year of Emperor Suinin's reign) was a prince of ancient {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} as recorded in historical texts such as the [[Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki]]. In the ''Nihon Shoki'', he is referred to as "Yamato-hiko no Mikoto," in the ''Kojiki'' as "'''Yamato-hiko no Mikoto''' (倭日子命)," and in other texts as "'''Yamato-hiko no Ōji''' (倭彦王子)"''Zoku Nihongi,'' Emperor Ten'ō, first year (781), entry for the 25th day of the 6th month.. He was a son of the 10th Emperor, {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}}, and a younger brother of Emperor [[:en:Suinin|Suinin]], the 11th Emperor. The ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki'' do not record any achievements or descendants but provide details regarding his funeral. == Records == According to the ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki,'' Yamato-hiko no Mikoto was born to {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}} and Empress [[Mimaki-hime]]. His elder brother was Ikumeiribikoisachi no Mikoto, later known as Emperor [[:en:Suinin|Suinin]]. The ''Nihon Shoki'' records that Yamato-hiko no Mikoto passed away on the 10th day of the 10th month in the 28th year of Emperor [[:en:Suinin|Suinin]]'s reign and was buried on the 2nd day of the 11th month at "Musano Tsukisaka." At his burial, attendants were interred alive around the grave. These individuals survived for several days, groaning continuously day and night, and their bodies were later scavenged by dogs and birds after death. Emperor [[:en:Suinin|Suinin]], moved by pity, issued a decree banning the practice of {{ill|Junshi|en|Junshi|WD=Q1071761|lt=Junshi|1=Junshi}} (ritual suicide) with the words, "Even if this is an ancient custom, what good is it if it is not proper?"{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }}. Furthermore, the ''Nihon Shoki'' mentions that when Empress [[Hibasu-hime|Hibasuhime no Mikoto]] died in the 32nd year of Emperor Suinin's reign, [[Nomi no Sukune]] suggested replacing human and animal sacrifices with clay objects for burial. This suggestion delighted the Emperor and became the origin of the practice of creating {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}} (clay figures), specifically the anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa used in burials.{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }}. The ''Kojiki'' briefly notes that at the burial of Yamato-hiko no Mikoto (倭日子命), human sacrifices were performed for the first time, stating, "At this king's funeral, a human fence was erected."{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }}. A similar tradition is recorded in the ''Zoku Nihongi'' under the year Ten'ō 1 (781). == Tomb == [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Tomb of Yamato-hiko no Mikoto
[[Masuyama Kofun]]
([[:en:Kashihara|Kashihara]], {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}})}]]] The [[Japanese imperial tombs|grave]] is designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} as ''Musano Tsukisaka no Haka'' (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) located in Toriya-chō, [[:en:Kashihara|Kashihara]], {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} The archaeological site name is [[Masuyama Kofun]]. The tomb is classified by the Imperial Household Agency as a square mound. According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', Yamato-hiko no Mikoto was buried at "Musano Tsukisaka," as noted above, but it is not listed in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' under the Bureau of Mausolea.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the stone chamber to the east of {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|ja|鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|qq=}} was also considered to be his grave.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} In April 1877, during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the Ministry of Home Affairs officially designated the current site as his grave, and the area was acquired along with the relocation of a shrine. Repairs began in 1890.{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}} However, modern research often questions the validity of this designation. == Analysis == The ''Nihon Shoki'' and ''Kojiki'' both associate Yamato-hiko no Mikoto with {{ill|Junshi|en|Junshi|WD=Q1071761|lt=Junshi|1=Junshi}} (ritual suicide). The ''Nihon Shoki'' refers to junshi as an "ancient custom," whereas the ''Kojiki'' describes it as "the first time a human fence was erected." There is some contradiction in these accounts.It is possible to resolve this contradiction by suggesting, as per the [[Kyushu hypothesis]], that Emperor [[:en:Suinin|Suinin]] adopted the precedent set by Himiko, despite it not being a Yamato tradition. Other historical references to junshi in Japan include a mention in the ''{{ill|Wajinden|en|Wajinden|WD=Q1135798|lt=Wei Zhi|1=Wajinden}}'' that over 100 slaves were sacrificed upon [[Himiko]]'s death and an imperial decree prohibiting junshi in the second year of {{ill|Taika (era)|en|Taika (era)|WD=Q1077930|lt=Taika|1=Taika (era)}} (646).{{Sfn|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }} The ''Nihon Shoki'' narrative should be regarded as a tale about the origins of {{ill|Representational Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|lt=anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Representational Haniwa|qq=}}, rather than about all {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}} (including {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}}). Archaeological evidence shows that {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} have roots in the {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|WD=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period|1=Yayoi period}} in the {{ill|Kibi Province|en|Kibi Province|WD=Q1155128|lt=Kibi region|1=Kingdom of Kibi}} through the use of specialized pedestals and jars, while anthropomorphic and shaped haniwa appear from the middle of the [[Kofun period]].[[Koichi Mori]] ''Invitation to the Tombs of Emperors (Chikuma Selections, No. 23)'', Chikuma Shobo, 2011, pp. 77-78. == Notes == {{Notelist}} {{Reflist}} == References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Musano Tsukisaka no Haka|title=[[Kokushi Daijiten (Showa Edition)|Kokushi Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=Yamato-hiko no Mikoto|title=Dictionary of Names of Ancient Japanese Clans, Standard Edition|publisher=[[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]]|isbn=978-4642014588|ref={{Harvid|倭彦命（古代氏族）|2010th year }}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}} * {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Prince Yamato-hiko no Mikoto, son of Emperor Sujin)] - Kashihara City Homepage, "Kashihara Explorer" {{translated page|ja|倭彦命|version=100295955|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:倭彦命]] {{wikidata link|Q11387083}} [[Category:Male imperial family members before the Kofun period]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]] [[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]]
